Making that configurable would be fine to me. The reason it updates the last digit of any tag is so that it sorts properly if you ever tag ANY higher version.

For example let's say you're currently on release v1.3.4, and you do some work and make a commit. With this option enabled, you'll get v1.3.5dev1. If you then decide to tag v1.3.5 or v1.4.0 or v2.0.0, they'll all properly sort after the dev version.

Now, if the option chose to give you 1.4.0dev1 instead, and you decided to tag a v1.3.5 because it really was just a patch fix, the dev version will sort as "newer".

The neat benefit of updating the last number is that it works with alpha, beta, and RC builds too. For example, let's say you are preparing for a big v2.0.0 release and want to put some alpha builds out there. You tag a v2.0.0a1 build, send it out to some people to test, and get some feedback about things to fix.  You fix the first bug, commit and build, and the dev option gives you a v2.0.0a2dev1 version. Make another change, commit and build, and you get v2.0.0a2dev2. Now you can tag a new alpha v2.0.0a2 and it'll sort correctly, or go ahead and tag v2.0.0, which will also sort correctly.

Overall, I would be happy with the "postX" functionality except that pip treats those versions as "real" releases compared to alpha, beta, rc, and dev "pre-releases", which you can avoid installing by not using the `--pre` flag.

I agree that this library should store the default as a reference so that if someone wants to shoot self in foot in the very pythonic manner described, they are free to do so. Disagree that we can have all the desired behavior if the implementation only touches the setter.

N.B. the example I provided was not a primary use-case, just an illustration of the danger of using a shared mutable value in a descriptor class like this. Even if it's not a usecase though, this object should treat state in a predictable and pythonic manner.

Let's talk interface though. Ergonomics. I think that explicit None passed to setter causing default to be set is bizarre. As a user, i'm expecting it will show up when the field is not set at all -- this is the meaning of the word 'default'.

Here are the behaviors I think seem sane:

a. If a schema obj is instantiated without assigning a specific value to a field, the default should show up.

```python
> wow = Wow()
> wow.foo
# {"wow": 1}
```

b. A field can be set to something else, then back to `None`, and the default should show up.

```python
> Wow(foo={"zip": "pow"})
> wow.foo
# {"zip": "pow"}
> Wow(foo=None)
> wow.foo
# {"wow": 1}
```

c. You can mutate the default value (but this shouldn't affect other instances)
```python
> wow2 = Wow()
> wow.foo['wow'] = 5
> wow.foo
# {"wow": 5}
> wow2.foo
# {"wow": 1}
```

d. Combining (b) and (c), there should be no 'memory' if the value is set to something else and then back to None. I.e. setting value to None gives a new reference to a fresh default value.
```python
> wow = Wow()
> wow.foo
# {"wow": 1}
> wow.foo['wow'] = 5
> wow.foo
# {"wow": 5}
> wow.foo = None
> wow.foo
# {"wow": 1}
```

There is a slight difference when using an explicitly empty **message field**:

```python
>>> from google.protobuf.struct_pb2 import ListValue
>>> from google.cloud.spanner_v1.proto.keys_pb2 import KeyRange
>>> k1 = KeyRange()
>>> k2 = KeyRange(
...     start_closed=ListValue(values=[]),
...     end_closed=ListValue(values=[]),
... )
>>> k1

>>> k2
start_closed {
}
end_closed {
}

>>>
```

As opposed to a simple field:

```python
>>> from google.cloud.spanner_v1.proto.keys_pb2 import KeySet
>>> k1 = KeySet()
>>> k2 = KeySet(all=False)
>>> k3 = KeySet(all=True)
>>> k1

>>> k2

>>> k3
all: true

>>>
```

A `sa.Float(precision=42)` (where 42 is not a mapped precision value) would be treated as a `sa.Float` by the `if type(typ) in revtypes` predicate.

A postgres `DOUBLE_PRECISION` would return a `Decimal(precision=42)`, having gone through the logic which this patch circumvents when the precision is 53.

I'm not sure what should be the correct logic when there is a precision but it is not 24 or 53.
A couple options:
1) If the precision is not None is not mapped, raise an exception (Something like `UnexpectedColumnPrecision`.)  If the precision is None, then use the mapping in `revtypes`.
e.g. `sa.Float(precision=42)` raises an exception; `sa.Float()` returns `float64` (The same dtype as `sa.Float`)
2) If the precision is not mapped, then just continue on with the previously defined dtype mapping logic . (This is the logic the PR currently implements.)
In this case, `sa.Float(precision=8)`, `sa.Float(precision=42)`, `sa.Float()` all return `float64`.

I lean towards raising the exception on unrecognized precisions.

> There will always be some configuration required for each API we generate.

Reactive corrections to problems identified in the generated library are fine. Configuration to be set (and reviewed) before the library can be generated is problematic, for being hard to scale.

> The cost of creating new handwritten client libraries is very high compared to the cost of manually setting this flag.

I'm not worried about adding a small cost to creating a new handwritten library. I'm worried about an engineer having to check whether a handwritten library exists or not before being able to autogenerate a library. We want to be able to generate libraries for hundreds of APIs (and, ideally, an unbound amount): every cost in the process needs to have that multiplier applied before considering its magnitude.

> There is a small, finite number of existing APIs that have handwritten client libraries, for which this flag needs to be set

So a machine-readable list should be easy to create? :)

But even so, I think the decision should be made at a later stage: Say Jun or a computer verifies that there's no hand-written library for this API today, and we proceed all the way with the generation. What happens when some time in the future they want to write a hand-written library on top?

First, do you think of the following code, to avoid misunderstanding on your remark :
```
try:
  os.remove(path)
except OSError as err:
  if err.errno in (errno.EACCESS, errno.EPERM)
    # Windows specific
    # We will not be able to remove a file before closing it.
    # To avoid race conditions described for Linux, we will not delete the lockfile,
    # just close it to be reused on the next Certbot call.
    pass
  else:
    raise
finally:
  os.close(fd)
```

But if we do not remove the lockfile, even if we release the lock, it will let some dirt in the directories processed by Certbot. Some Python libraries around there seems to have solved the issue both for Linux and Windows. I have already some work done for the next PR about the locking mechanism.

I think we can talk more about that in the relevant PR, but here is an excerpt: while Linux handles that correctly, the current locking process in Certbot leads to resource leak on Windows when Certbot finishes in a unexpectedly state. On AppVeyor. the VM is simply stale forever because the file and its lock are never released after failed tests that invoke the lock mechanism. I would like to construct a plateform-independent context manager for lock file, similar to thread locks or file descriptos and their `with` statement approach, fall-backed by the existing `atexit` in Certbot, to ensure that no lock or file descriptor ever leak for any exit state.

I'm personally not a fan of the idea that you should wrap standard library functions in a language just to provide a better name. Standard library functions are well, standard. Even if the name is bad, people get used to them and learn how to use them as they gain more experience in the language.

We may not have written them much ourselves before now, but I think using these methods is extremely common and it's hard to do anything meaningful in Python 3 without them. We currently have 113 uses of `encode` or `decode` in Certbot.

I personally think writing a custom function when calling a (common) standard library function will do hurts readability more than it helps. Readers have to go check out the definition of your custom functions to see what they're doing only to find out the answer is not much.

> Very often, particularly when trying to use the various crypto libraries.
